blurb: A low Front Range foothills native forming neat glossy evergreen rosettes that throw up clouds of small blue-violet flowers in late spring; happy in dry shade and lean granitic soil.
sun: Full sun to part shade (takes dry shade)
seasons: Clouds of blue-violet flowers late spring–early summer; glossy evergreen basal rosette year-round

shots.cap: Late spring — flower close-up · © Jared Shorma (CC-BY) / iNaturalist
Late spring — flowering mass · © dlehle (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Habit — growing on granite · © Elias (CC-BY) / iNaturalist

care.hardiness: Very cold-hardy — a Front Range foothills native (Colorado/Wyoming, ~5,250–9,850 ft) good to USDA zone 4 and colder, so it shrugs off Boulder's 5b–6a winters and is perfectly at home in our climate.[2][3]
care.planting: Set out container plants in spring after the mid-May average last frost, or in early fall, into lean gritty soil on a sunny slope. In pots use a fast-draining cactus/grit mix and keep it on the dry side — potted crowns rot far more readily than plants in the ground.[1][3]
care.sun: Full sun to light shade — at least six hours of sun for the best, brightest bloom; it naturally grows in foothills forest openings, so it takes a little afternoon shade.[1][3]
care.soil: Needs sharp drainage; though adaptable, like all penstemons it is healthier in well-drained, lean ground and rots in Boulder's heavy wet clay — plant in gritty soil and do NOT enrich with compost, which shortens its life.[1][3]
care.water: Xeric — low water and drought-tolerant once established; it wants spring moisture to bloom but overwatering (especially after flowering) causes crown rot, so keep summer irrigation minimal.[1]
care.spacing: Space about 8–12 in apart; it stays low — roughly 6–12 in tall — and spreads into a low mat that enlarges each year.[2][3]
care.propagation: Grow from seed with cold-moist stratification (fall-sow outdoors for natural chilling, or chill moist in the fridge before a late-winter start). Penstemons resent division — don't split the crown; propagate from basal/softwood cuttings instead.[1][3]
care.stratify: Cold-moist stratify the seed (fall sowing supplies natural winter chilling) to break dormancy.[1][3]
care.bloom: Low spikes of pale sky-blue to lavender-purple tubular flowers (with a lavender throat) in late spring to early summer, about May–July on the Front Range; deadhead to tidy and prolong.[1][2][3]
care.feeding: None needed — penstemons take no fertilizer in ordinary soil, and feeding only makes weak growth and shortens lifespan.[1]
care.maintenance: Low: deadhead after bloom, ease off summer water to avoid crown rot, and leave the low evergreen mat over winter; clean up spent stems in late fall or early spring.[1]
care.troubles: Few problems beyond crown/root rot in wet, poorly drained or amended soil; occasional grasshoppers, flea beetles and rust.[1]
care.companions: A premier low xeric native for the front of dry sunny beds and rock gardens, valued for native bees — pair with other foothills natives and grasses.[2]

references.name: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Penstemon virens

edible.summary: Grown ornamentally as a native pollinator plant — no documented edible use.[2]
edible.caution: No known culinary use; not documented as poisonous, but it is not a food plant — don't eat it.[2]
